text: I usually have toast with butter and marmalade, maybe some scrambled eggs if I'm feeling peckish. If my grandkids come round, we'll sometimes make pancakes together – always a fun morning activity!

I usually make myself a simple sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, perhaps some soup or a salad. If I'm feeling more like treating myself, my daughter will sometimes bring me something from her place or we'll meet out at our local café for a bite to eat. Sometimes, too, I might pop into the kitchen to whip up a batch of scones or biscuits – there's nothing quite like having freshly baked goods to brighten up an afternoon! A cuppa tea is always part of the deal, of course. It's all very low-key, really; just fuel for the rest of the morning before heading off to whatever other plans the day has in store.
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I try to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. It helps me feel rested and refreshed for the morning. Sometimes, though, my body might tell me otherwise!

I believe a bit of self-care is essential, even though my husband used to tease me about being too busy before he...well, anyway. Nowadays, I try to make sure I'm getting enough rest, eating decent food, and staying hydrated throughout the day. A warm cuppa first thing in the morning sets the tone nicely. If I have some free time, I love tending to my little vegetable patch out in the backyard – there's something therapeutic about watching those seeds sprout into actual plants! On Wednesdays, our local library offers a lovely session where we discuss classic novels; it's always great catching up with the others and sharing thoughts. Afternoons might be more subdued, so I'll take an hour-long walk along the nearby canal path to clear my mind and stretch my legs. Evenings involve making dinner and maybe having a chat with one of the grandkids via video call – brings us all closer together. Of course, consistency isn't always easy, given my energy dips mid-day, but these simple habits really help keep me feeling relatively grounded despite everything else going on.

I think networking is a bit like tending to my garden - you need to nurture relationships and make sure everyone knows who's been contributing what. At work, I was very much involved in various committees and groups across different departments. For instance, our local health authority had an annual conference where we'd bring together all sorts of professionals from hospitals and clinics; those were always great opportunities to meet people from other areas and share best practices. Even though I'm fully retired now, I still try to attend some of these events whenever I can. And with my grandkids being young adults, it's lovely getting them involved too – sometimes they'll come along with me! They're quite chatty, so it's amusing watching us strike up conversations with others while they chat away. At my workplace during my retirement, although I didn't really have any official position anymore, I made an effort to remain visible, keeping in touch with former colleagues and attending meetings occasionally. It kept me feeling part of the team, even though I wasn't officially employed there anymore.

To be honest, it takes some discipline, but I find setting a regular routine really helps me stay focused while working from home. My peak productivity is usually between 8 o'clock in the morning until noon, so I try to make the most of those four hours. I'll plan out my tasks for the day beforehand, break them down into smaller chunks, and stick to a schedule. It's amazing how much you can accomplish during that window! I also take breaks to refresh myself – perhaps grab a cuppa, stretch, or go outside for some fresh air. And, of course, having a tidy workspace makes all the difference; I like being able to close the door and focus without distractions. It's funny, people always think staying at home should mean being lazy, but trust me, there's still work to be done! Setting boundaries, even when working from home, ensures I'm getting everything sorted. Plus, knowing exactly what needs doing each day gives me peace of mind. Now, let's have another cup of tea...

I'm still getting used to them, to be honest! My son is always saying how easy they make everything, but I have to admit, sometimes I feel like I'm missing out on some of the nuances of a face-to-face conversation. Still, he's been very helpful, showing me how to work my tablet and whatnot. Now we can catch up regularly and talk about our grandkids. It's nice having him nearby, even if I am getting a bit frustrated with all these gadgets myself...

I find a bit of discipline is essential even outside of work hours, you know. For me, setting achievable daily targets helps keep me focused. It could be tidying my garden, writing letters to my grandkids, or simply paying bills – something productive to look forward to. Having some sort of plan gives me a sense of accomplishment and makes the transition into retirement feel more manageable. Plus, having set routines allows me to make sure everything runs smoothly while keeping an eye on finances, which was always a priority during my working years. A bit of personal responsibility never hurts!

I'm a planner, so I have to admit it takes some adjusting when priorities shift unexpectedly. In my previous job, we'd usually set out clear objectives and deadlines, which helped me stay focused. But sometimes circumstances changed – perhaps a key member left the team or funding was reduced – and suddenly our priority list got revised overnight. To adapt, I made sure to communicate openly with colleagues and management. We reassessed what needed doing and prioritised based on urgency and importance. It wasn't always easy, but having regular meetings and being transparent kept everyone informed. Sometimes, we had to let go of smaller projects to focus on more critical ones; that way, we could still deliver results even though our scope narrowed. With experience, you develop your flexibility and learn how to manage multiple priorities effectively.

I think being a strong communicator has been key in advocating for myself throughout my career. As an experienced admin assistant, I always made sure to be very specific about what needed to be done, how it should be done, and why. If there was any issue or concern, I'd speak directly to whoever needed to know, whether it was a colleague or manager. And I never hesitated to ask questions if something wasn't quite right – asking clarifying questions shows willingness to listen, which is essential, don't you think? Nowadays, even though I'm fully retired, I still try to apply those habits. In our local community groups where we have meetings and decisions need making, I'll stand up and share my thoughts if I feel strongly about something. It's all about having your say, isn't it? Of course, some people might find me a bit forceful at times, but I believe that assertiveness helps ensure that everyone's voice is heard. My motto is "patience and curiosity" getting you far more than rushing through life; so sometimes taking a moment to explain and justify one's position seems like common sense!

I think when I feel like I'm being misunderstood, my first instinct is usually to try and clarify what was said or done. Sometimes I'll ask someone to explain themselves again, or seek out more information so we're all on the same page. If needed, I might take a bit of time to collect myself before responding, to make sure I respond thoughtfully rather than reacting impulsively. But honestly, I suppose some people tend to misjudge me because I am quite direct and straight-forward in how I communicate. My husband used to tease me about being too blunt sometimes! But I believe being honest and open helps avoid misunderstandings down the line. Of course, there have been occasions where I wish others had understood something sooner... but overall, I reckon getting clarity is key. Patience and persistence usually serve me well in those moments.

I always try to be present and engage with everyone, even though my husband is no longer here with us. We have a lovely time catching up with our grown-up children and grandkids. They're such an important part of my life now. When we gather together, I usually spend some time chatting with them individually, asking how their lives are going and sharing stories about mine. The kids love hearing about my adventures in the garden – they think it's hilarious when I regale them with tales of failed experiments gone wrong! We always make sure there's plenty of food and drink available, so people can graze throughout the gathering. My famous scones and jam tarts are always a hit with the young ones, while the adults appreciate a warm cuppa and a slice of cake. It's wonderful seeing everyone laughing and having fun together. Sometimes we'll play games like Scrabble or Dominos, which brings out the competitive side of me! If I'm being completely honest, I do tend to take charge of making sure everything runs smoothly and according to plan. Old habits die hard, I suppose! But overall, those family gatherings bring immense joy to my heart; it feels truly special knowing I'm surrounded by loved ones who accept me unconditionally.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on all these new-fangled platforms like LinkedIn. Don't really know why everyone makes such a fuss about them, and I find most people just post what they think others might want to hear rather than genuine thoughts. In my line of work, networking was more about face-to-face conversations and building relationships, which is something I still value highly today. Now that I'm retired, I'd much prefer a simple website where I could share some bits about myself and connect with acquaintances and loved ones directly – nothing fancy, mind! Just a way to say hello every now and then.

I think sensitivity is key when discussing difficult subjects. As a result of having lost my husband recently, I'm all too aware how painful conversations can be. When approaching someone who may already feel vulnerable, one should tread softly, listen attentively, and try to empathise as much as possible. I believe being genuine, honest yet compassionate helps to create an open atmosphere where people feel encouraged to express themselves freely. Sometimes, avoiding certain issues altogether might prevent unnecessary distress, while other times facing them straight away allows individuals to process their feelings more effectively. It really depends on knowing the individual involved and considering what will best suit the situation, I suppose.

I'd say I'm fairly resistant to peer pressure, actually. As a child, my upbringing was quite traditional, so we were taught to stand our ground and think critically rather than follow the crowd blindly. My mother always said, "It's better to be different, not popular." And, all these years later, I still find myself valuing being true to oneself more than keeping up appearances. Now, of course, there might be certain... let's call them'social expectations', where people expect me to fit into a particular mould or join in on something simply because everyone else is doing it. But I try to remind myself why those choices aren't really for me. It's easy to fall prey to the desire to avoid conflict or save face, but ultimately, staying authentic feels like the best way forward in the end. That being said, I am human, and sometimes, perhaps even occasionally, I may yield to peer influence – usually when someone shares a valid point or shows genuine concern for my well-being. After all, having close relationships is an essential part of life! So while I wouldn't describe myself as overly sensitive or easily swayed, I definitely value open dialogue and mutual respect in any decision-making process.

I'm rather wary of those targeted advertisements, to be honest. It seems like every website knows my name, what I buy, where I live... sometimes it feels a bit too intrusive, if you ask me. I try to stick to trusted websites and avoid clicking on any unfamiliar links. And whenever I sign up for something, I make sure I read the terms and conditions properly so I know exactly how they'll be using my data. Still, I suppose some personalisation is nice, isn't it? As long as it's done sensibly and not in an overbearing way.

To be honest, I'm not very tech-savvy, so I tend to stick with what I know. My granddaughter has helped me set up my tablet a bit better over the past couple of years. Now, I usually have one tab open for emails, maybe another for news or some Wikipedia articles, and sometimes I'll open an online recipe website while I'm looking something up. But once those start cluttering up too much, I find myself getting frustrated and closing them all out – it's easier like that! A simple system works best for me, thank you for asking.

I try to ignore them really. As a simple lady who likes her peace, there's little point in engaging with someone who wants to provoke a reaction out of me. If necessary, I'll block them or report them to the platform, but generally speaking, I find it best to focus on my own world rather than getting drawn into their negativity. A bit like when your grandkids used to argue among themselves – eventually, they'd run out of steam and move on. Same principle applies here, I suppose!

I usually try calling my grandson first because he's always been quite handy with these sorts of things. If we're having trouble getting a connection, he'll walk me through what might have gone wrong and how to fix it myself. It's lovely that he's so helpful like that. If that doesn't work, I may end up asking someone else – perhaps one of his siblings is more tech-savvy too! Sometimes, though, I find myself getting frustrated and wondering why all these complicated contraptions need to be so finicky...

I like to think my "waste-not, want-not" upbringing has stuck with me! I make a point to review our household finances every month, create a budget based on income and expenses, and stick to it as closely as possible. We have a separate account specifically for bill payments so we can see everything laid out clearly. Once I've planned out what needs to be done, I set reminders on my calendar to ensure all bills are covered before the due dates come around. And, of course, I try to avoid putting them off until the last minute – there's always something else that seems more pressing, isn't there? It helps that my son still checks in regularly now that he's an adult, making sure his mum is managing okay financially. He knows how much I hate getting into financial difficulties. Plus, since retiring, I'm grateful for having some extra time to focus on keeping track of everything. Still, I know I'd struggle if I had any significant health issues or other major changes affecting my daily routine... but overall, it works quite well for us.

I always try to think about whether I really need something before committing to a subscription service. If I haven't used it for months, chances are I'll probably forget about it anyway, so there's little point paying for it anymore. Occasionally, my grandkids will remind me of something we signed up for together, like an app or streaming platform, and I might end up keeping it because they're still using it. But generally, I'm quite ruthless – if I feel I've gotten value out of it, great; otherwise, goodbye! It's all part of being mindful of our finances, you know.

Well, before making a purchase like replacing my washing machine, I always try to think very clearly about whether I really need something or not. It's funny, I'm one of those people who still likes to weigh up the pros and cons even now, all these years later! My late husband used to tease me about being too thrifty, but I reckon he was just joking because we were brought up with such strong values about saving money. I usually make a list of what I'd be getting rid of – what else could I buy instead? Do I have enough storage space already? Would an older model work just fine? Then, if everything seems okay, I'll check prices online to find deals or discounts. Sometimes, our grandkids will give us advice, saying "Grandma/Mummy, you should get [this brand/model]!" And while I appreciate the enthusiasm, I take it all into consideration myself. It might sound silly, but there is some satisfaction in finding a bargain or negotiating a price, knowing I've managed to save a bit more money. Of course, sometimes things break or wear out unexpectedly, so having some buffer left over isn't entirely bad either! Ultimately, though, once I feel fairly certain I've made a sensible decision, I trust

I always try to think twice before making a purchase decision. If something is still in decent condition and serves its purpose, I'll usually opt for second-hand first – there's so much waste out there and I'm all about being thrifty! But if it's an essential item like my favourite books or some gardening equipment, I might treat myself to brand-new if it's really needed. It depends on what will make me happy in the end. My late husband was quite keen on keeping everything shiny and new, whereas I believe simplicity has its charms too.

Well, I'm a bit of an old-fashioned soul, but my husband always used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned". You know, waste not, want not! We were brought up that way during the war, so we never really thought much about spending money unless absolutely necessary. And even now, I find myself being quite careful with our finances. We have a simple rule: whenever there's something we need or want, we weigh the costs against what we already have, and try to make ends meet first. If we're still unsure, I think ahead to how likely it'll be worth the expense later on – like buying seeds for the garden instead of food, which might save us money in the long run. My son says I'm being thrifty, but I reckon it's better to be safe! My late husband was very supportive; he'd remind me that every little saving adds up, and before you know it, those pennies add up into pounds and shillings... er, dollars! He encouraged me to set aside some each month in a separate pot, knowing it wouldn't go unnoticed. Now that he's gone, I take extra care to stick to his advice. It feels sensible, helps me feel secure. If someone

I try to plan ahead during those periods. My son is always telling me about these great deals he finds online, so we make a list together of what we need to buy, like winter coats, warm jumpers, or summer clothes. We shop wisely, taking advantage of discounts whenever we spot them. It makes sense to wait until the prices drop rather than paying full price. Plus, I love having something to look forward to, even if it's just a bargain. And let's be honest, I'm a bit of a saver – I hate wasting money!

Portion control is really quite simple for me, thank you for asking! As a child growing up during wartime rationing, we were taught to make do with what little food we had, so it was second nature to be mindful of serving sizes even back then. Now, while I still love indulging in my favorite baked goods like cakes and biscuits, I try to pace myself and stop before I fill up too much. It's all about balance, isn't it? When cooking for others, whether it's at work or for our grandkids, I always aim to serve generous portions but avoid going overboard. And of course, when eating out, I'm happy to take leftovers home for another meal – there's nothing worse than wasting perfectly good food! It's funny, though; now that I'm older, I find I have more trouble resisting those late-night snacks when I should probably stick to bed instead... But overall, moderation has been key throughout my life, and I think it's helped me develop a healthier relationship with food altogether.

I think a therapist should be someone who listens attentively and is genuinely interested in understanding why one feels certain way. You know, not just there to offer solutions or tell you what to do, but really try to grasp the underlying reasons behind our thoughts and feelings. It sounds rather obvious now, but some people seem so focused on fixing issues before exploring them properly. For me, finding out "what's going on" seems like a vital part of getting better. And I suppose having an open mind and being able to share my story freely helps too – though sometimes I find myself hesitating because I'm worried others might judge me!

I think prevention is key, really. For me, making sure I have a balance between work and personal life has been crucial. As someone who's used to being quite busy, even in my retirement, I find it essential to set aside dedicated time for myself each day, whether that's going for a walk, tending to my garden, or simply enjoying some quiet time with a cuppa. It sounds simple, but those little moments add up. Also, staying hydrated, eating healthily, and getting enough rest – all these contribute to feeling more energised throughout the day. And, of course, having something enjoyable to look forward to helps too; like my regular visit with grandkids or a lovely afternoon spent browsing the local market. It might sound old-fashioned, but there's value in taking breaks during the day, perhaps stepping outside into fresh air or switching gears completely – sometimes, recharging takes an hour, other times, it's just five minutes. The point is finding what works best for your lifestyle and sticking to it. Oh, and one thing I always say to others: "Patience and curiosity" go much further than rushing through life! You'd be surprised how many people underestimate the power of slowing down and savoring every moment.

I think a combination of establishing a relaxing bedtime routine and making sure my surroundings conducive to rest has helped me improve my sleep quality. You know, setting aside some quiet time each evening before bed to unwind – perhaps having a cuppa and reading a book or practicing gentle stretches – really helps signal to my brain that it's time to wind down. And getting ready for bed at the same reasonable hour every night seems to have made all the difference, too. Oh, and avoiding screens for an hour or so beforehand is quite helpful; those blue lights seem to interfere with melatonin production, don't they? Simple changes like these little habits have made a noticeable improvement, which is lovely!

I try to mix it up a bit, really. You know, too much repetition can be dull. On Mondays and Tuesdays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays, I tend to stick to my regular walk round the block, which is usually about half an hour long. It helps me get some fresh air and stretch my legs before the afternoon slump sets in. On Fridays, though, I like to take a slightly longer walk – maybe 45 minutes or so – sometimes exploring local parks or nature reserves near my place. And at the weekend, I might join forces with my neighbour for a couple of miles along the towpath alongside our canal. Nothing strenuous, mind – we're not exactly marathon runners! Just something gentle to keep us moving. If I'm feeling particularly energetic, I'll squeeze in some yoga poses while watching telly during commercial breaks; nothing fancy, just a little flexibility never hurts, eh? It's all about finding balance, isn't it? Too much sitting, and I start to feel rather creaky...

When evaluating different solution options, I tend to think step-by-step, weighing the pros and cons rather than jumping straight into action. It sounds a bit methodical, perhaps, but I find it helps me make more informed decisions. For instance, let's say there were several companies offering retirement plans – I'd consider factors like fees, flexibility, investment options, and customer service before making my choice. And, if necessary, I might even consult with someone else who has experience in those areas. My mind tends to focus on why something is best suited for me personally, rather than what others tell me. Does that sound sensible?

I think incorporating feedback is a vital part of any solution, really. When someone takes the time to share their thoughts or concerns, it shows me there's value in what they have to say. So, first thing I'd like to thank them for taking the time to provide that feedback – please know your input has been heard! When considering those comments, I try to take a step back, gather my thoughts, and re-evaluate our proposed plan. Sometimes, I might even revisit previous conversations we had on the topic before diving deeper into how that specific piece of feedback impacts our overall strategy. In particular, I'm quite keen on addressing the root cause rather than just treating symptoms. By doing so, we increase the chances of creating more sustainable changes. This way, whatever suggestions were made become an integral component of the final decision-making process. Now, while giving consideration to others' viewpoints, I always aim to strike a balance between being open to innovative ideas and making sure everyone involved understands why certain choices will be taken. It's essential to weigh the pros and cons together and make informed decisions based on shared priorities. Ultimately, once all factors have been thoughtfully weighed, I'll work towards implementing adjustments where necessary, keeping full transparency throughout each stage of implementation. And remember,

I think my approach is quite methodical and step-by-step. I like to break things down into smaller parts, consider all sides of an argument before forming a conclusion, and make sure there aren't any obvious solutions that have been overlooked. It helps me feel more grounded and certain about what I'm saying. Sometimes people tell me I'm too indecisive because I weigh everything so heavily, but I suppose that's part of being a bit of a perfectionist!

I find that writing things down helps me to process my thoughts and organize them better. When I'm faced with a complex issue or decision, I like to jot down key points and concerns, then review them afterwards. Sometimes, talking through what I've written with someone else will give me clarity too – whether that's a grandchild, a close friend, or even myself in conversation. It really is saying "the why" that gets me going, rather than just listing facts out; there has to be an underlying reason behind everything we're discussing.

I think identifying areas for growth is quite a process for me, actually. You know, I'm a bit like my grandmother used to say, "A penny saved is a penny earned." But sometimes I find myself wondering what other investments could be made – whether that's in personal development or simply being more efficient in everyday life. For instance, with all the changes happening in our world today, I feel like there must be so many opportunities to improve ourselves, to adapt and become better versions of who we already are. And yet, I have to admit that I still struggle with keeping up-to-date with new technologies, even though everyone seems to be using them all the time! It makes me chuckle; I remember getting frustrated with those computer classes I had to take in secretarial college because I was so out of touch. Another area where I'd love some improvement is perhaps becoming more digitally savvy. While I manage to navigate most aspects of digital life pretty well, there are certain intricacies that seem to stump me. My grandsons always tease me about asking too many questions, which might make it sound silly, but honestly, it's reassuring knowing someone like them can guide me along! Lastly, while I value tradition and familiarity above all else, having been married for such

I think my strategy is quite simple really. You know, I've found that a bit of caution never hurts, so I tend to take things slowly and consider all angles before jumping into something completely new. But once I feel like I have enough information and an idea of what's involved, I try to be open-minded and give it a go. And I always say that patience and curiosity can get you much farther than rushing through life. It sounds daft, perhaps, but it seems to work for me!

I make sure to prioritise my reading, really. As a retiree, I have more free time now, so I try to utilise it wisely. In the morning, before getting started with other daily chores, I like to sit quietly with a cuppa and some reading material – usually historical fiction or biographies. The quieter it is, the better I feel! My husband used to always say I was most productive during those first couple of hours of the day. And of course, I take advantage of any breaks I get throughout the day, too. Whether it's while waiting in line or sitting on the bus, I'll pull out my e-reader or bring along a paperback. It helps me unwind, relax and escape into another world. Plus, it keeps my mind sharp, which is essential for an oldie like myself!

I tend to find out about new authors or genres through word-of-mouth recommendations from people close to me, like my son, his wife, or our mutual friends. Sometimes I'll pick up a book based on its cover design or reviews from reputable sources, like The Guardian or The Times Literary Supplement. Occasionally, I might stumble upon something while browsing in a second-hand bookstore or library sale – there's still magic in discovering hidden gems among those shelves! But really, most importantly, I rely on being asked what books others have enjoyed; I love discussing literature with fellow readers and getting tailored suggestions.

I find myself often tidying away any clutter or organizing my workspace while listening to music, usually classical or traditional folk tunes. It helps me focus and gets everything sorted out before a busy morning. Occasionally, I might be sewing something or fixing up the garden while listening to music; there's something soothing about having some gentle sounds playing in the background.

I must say, I'm a bit of an auditory learner myself. When I have some time to kill during my daily commute, either to work or out for a walk, I find I thoroughly enjoy listening to podcasts or audiobooks. It's wonderful how something like music isn't really available when one is actively engaging physically; however, being able to absorb information while still using both hands has been quite liberating! Audiobooks allow me to make the most of downtime, whether it be sitting quietly on my sofa waiting for visitors, riding the bus into town, or even tending to my vegetable patch – whatever suits best for that particular moment. As long as there's someone narrating, providing interesting insights, and keeping my mind occupied, I think I'm perfectly happy.

I love staying active! You know, people say retirement is all relaxation, but that isn't really true for me. There's always something to be done, whether it's tending to my garden or getting out and seeing what's going on locally. My grandkids come round regularly, so we have a lovely time playing games together or having picnics in the park. In fact, one thing I'm looking forward to soon is taking part in an organised gardening course – maybe even growing some exotic plants! It'll give me chance to meet like-minded folk and pick up some tips from experts. Nothing beats being outdoors though; there's nothing quite like feeling the sun on your face and watching those little seeds sprout into beautiful blooms. Occasionally, I might pop into the local community centre to join in with whatever activity's available – usually a bit of exercise class or perhaps attending a workshop. Those sorts of things keep me sociable while still keeping me moving. And let's not forget books! A good novel or biography always seems to transport me somewhere else and gets my imagination running wild. So, yes, staying active has become such an essential part of my daily routine now – keeps me young, happy, and fulfilled.

I really value my time alone, actually. It's a chance for me to recharge and think clearly, without any distractions. You know, people tend to assume that being an extrovert is necessary for enjoyment, but I'm happy to spend hours curled up with a good book or tending to my garden. There's something peaceful about sitting quietly, surrounded by nature, watching the world go by. Of course, it's lovely to have company sometimes too – like spending time with loved ones or attending local events – but there's definitely something special about having some space all to myself.

When trying a new cuisine, I'm always excited to discover something different, so I like to start with a dish I think might be relatively accessible. Maybe one my grandkids have mentioned is popular, or a recipe we've seen on television. If it seems simple enough, I'll give it a go. Sometimes, I ask them to help me prepare it – they're great at measuring out ingredients! They love being involved, and it makes the process more enjoyable. Even if it turns out okay, rather than perfect, there's usually some element of satisfaction knowing we tried something new together. And who knows, sometimes it becomes a favourite!

I think creating variety is key to keeping me going, even though some might say I'm a bit set in my ways! *laughs* For one thing, I try to mix between indoor and outdoor activities – whether it's tending to my vegetable patch out in the garden, which always brings something fresh air into my daily routine, or sitting indoors enjoying a cuppa while sorting through an interesting book or magazine. In terms of specific hobbies, I love getting together with fellow readers at our local book club; we all meet monthly to discuss whatever novel or biography has piqued us since the last meeting. It's great chatting with like-minded people who share similar tastes in literature. Occasionally, too, I'll take part in some charity work at our community center – nothing major, really, but it helps remind me there's still so much to give back, even at my stage in life. If I have free time during the weekdays, I usually plan ahead and squeeze in some gardening, either pruning plants or planting seeds for future harvests. If the weather permits, I may join some mates for a walk along the nearby canal, watching birds, watching the world go by... these little breaks always refresh me. Lastly, cooking for loved ones does bring joy too.

I'm quite particular about keeping track of my free time, even though some might think me a bit old-fashioned! I like jotting notes in a notebook whenever something comes to mind – be it what I read, who we have for dinner, or plans made with friends. It helps me feel more organised and ensures nothing falls through the cracks. Occasionally, I'll glance through last month's or so entries to remind myself how much I've been enjoying our little book club meetings or how many nice walks I took during the summer months. Simple pleasures make such an impact on one's mental health, don't you agree?

I'm quite particular about keeping my space tidy and organized. I like having a bit of routine, so every morning I make sure to plan out my day, prioritise what needs to be done, and check my list before starting any tasks. This helps me stay focused and avoid feeling overwhelmed. My trusty calendar is always filled with reminders, meetings, and appointments – either on paper or digitally, whichever suits best. And of course, there's nothing more satisfying than ticking off completed tasks! My grandkids even love seeing how many items we tick off together during our daily checks. For personal records and paperwork, I have designated folders labeled clearly and colour-coded, which makes them easy to find when needed. I try to sort documents into categories whenever I receive something – bills, receipts, letters... and update everything regularly so nothing gets lost or misplaced. In addition to these methods, I also value simple tools like sticky notes, to-do lists, and a decent planner to jot down quick notes throughout the day. It might seem old-fashioned, but sometimes those little scribbled notes prove invaluable! Overall, while technology has certainly changed the way people work, some tried-and-tested practices never go astray, and I believe a combination of traditional techniques works really well for me

I like starting my day a bit later nowadays. After getting dressed, I make myself a cuppa tea, sit down, and spend some time checking emails, responding to any urgent messages, and catching up on local news via my BBC radio app – always seems to be an easy way to stay informed without too much fuss. Then I take a quick glance out into the garden while enjoying my tea; usually check how everything is looking outside, making sure nothing untoward has happened overnight. Once done, I grab my bag, add what I need, and set off onto my daily commute using our local bus service. It takes me about half an hour to reach town where I have various places to visit such as the post office, library, and sometimes meetings with acquaintances or grandkids who live nearby. Sometimes we might pop into a café for something tasty for breakfast – although I try to stick to simple fare. By midmorning, I'm usually ready for another productive session!

I try to be mindful of my usage, turning lights out when not needed and using thermal curtains during winter. We have double glazing, which helps a lot. In summer, we open our windows slightly at night to let the cool air in before closing them again during the day. My grandkids like sitting outside while we watch telly together; it makes me think twice about heating the living room too much! Simple measures make a difference, and I find it's all about finding a balance between comfort and saving money.

To be honest, I'm still getting used to them all. My son helped me set one up a bit, but now he's always saying "Mum, why didn't you figure out how to turn on the lights?" You know! It's funny. The main thing is, I love having some assistance, like telling Alexa what room to play music in, so I only have to give instructions once rather than fiddling around. But sometimes I feel like there must be an easier way...I mean, my brain gets muddled enough already! Still, it's nice to not have to remember everything myself. Just wish they could make them more user-friendly for someone who's not too tech-savvy, like me!

I think prioritising is all about weighing what's most important to me against my limited capacity and resources. For instance, since becoming a widow six years ago, I have more freedom to focus on myself now. So, making sure I'm keeping fit, spending quality time with my grandkids, and enjoying some hobbies like gardening come top. On the other hand, there are still responsibilities – managing my finances, staying organised, looking after my health... those need balancing too. When faced with multiple demands, I try breaking them down into manageable chunks. If one task takes longer, I might put others on hold temporarily until I complete it. And generally speaking, once something becomes urgent, I make space for it, even if it means adjusting smaller plans. It's really about finding common ground between individual priorities and being flexible enough to adjust course when needed. Simple solutions usually work best for me; getting bogged down in complicated decisions tends to cause unnecessary stress!
